sections.title: Native Korean Numbers
sections.explanation: Korean has two number systems, and both are used in daily life. The native Korean system (고유어 수) is used for counting things, telling hours, and ages. The numbers 1-10 are: 하나 (1), 둘 (2), 셋 (3), 넷 (4), 다섯 (5), 여섯 (6), 일곱 (7), 여덟 (8), 아홉 (9), 열 (10). For 11-19, combine 열 with the unit: 열하나 (11), 열둘 (12), etc. Tens are: 스물 (20), 서른 (30), 마흔 (40), 쉰 (50), 예순 (60), 일흔 (70), 여든 (80), 아흔 (90). Important: when 하나, 둘, 셋, 넷, and 스물 appear before a counter, they shorten to 한, 두, 세, 네, and 스무: 한 개 (one thing), 두 명 (two people), 세 마리 (three animals). Native Korean numbers are typically used only up to 99; beyond that, Sino-Korean numbers take over.
sections.tip: Remember: 하나, 둘, 셋, 넷 shorten to 한, 두, 세, 네 before counters. The rhyme 하둘셋넷 (ha-dul-set-net) is how Koreans count off quickly, like '1-2-3-4!'

examples.en: three apples (세 = shortened 셋 before counter 개)
25 years old (스물 + 다섯; 스물 keeps its full form here because the shortened form 스무 is only used directly before a counter, as in 스무 살 = 20 years old)

exercises.prompt: What is the native Korean number for 7?
exercises.explanation: 일곱 is 7 in native Korean. 칠 is 7 in Sino-Korean. 여섯 is 6 in native Korean.

exercises.prompt: How does 하나 change before a counter?
exercises.explanation: Before a counter, 하나 shortens to 한: 한 개 (one thing), 한 명 (one person), 한 잔 (one cup). The same pattern applies to 둘→두, 셋→세, 넷→네, 스물→스무.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 12 in native Korean?
exercises.explanation: In the native Korean system, 12 is 열 (10) + 둘 (2) = 열둘. 십이 is 12 in the Sino-Korean system.

sections.title: Sino-Korean Numbers
sections.explanation: The Sino-Korean number system (한자어 수) comes from Chinese characters and is used for dates, money, phone numbers, addresses, minutes, and numbers above 99. The digits are: 일 (1), 이 (2), 삼 (3), 사 (4), 오 (5), 육 (6), 칠 (7), 팔 (8), 구 (9), 십 (10). Larger numbers are built systematically: 십일 (11 = 10+1), 이십 (20 = 2×10), 이십삼 (23 = 2×10+3), 백 (100), 천 (1,000), 만 (10,000). Note that Korean groups large numbers by 만 (ten thousands) rather than by thousands as in English: 50,000 is 오만, not 'fifty thousand.' Prices use Sino-Korean numbers: 삼천 원 (3,000 won), 만 오천 원 (15,000 won). Dates also use Sino-Korean: 삼월 십오일 (March 15th).
sections.tip: Use Sino-Korean for: dates (월, 일), money (원), phone numbers, minutes (분), and any number over 99. An easy way to remember: if it involves a formal system or measurement, it is probably Sino-Korean.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 500 in Sino-Korean?
exercises.explanation: 500 is 오 (5) + 백 (100) = 오백. 다섯 is the native Korean 5 and would not be combined with 백. 오십 is 50.

exercises.prompt: Which number system is used for money in Korean?
exercises.explanation: Money always uses Sino-Korean numbers: 천 원 (1,000 won), 만 원 (10,000 won), 오만 원 (50,000 won). Native Korean numbers are not used with currency.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 45,000 in Sino-Korean?
exercises.explanation: 45,000 = 사만 (4 × 10,000) + 오천 (5 × 1,000) = 사만 오천. Korean groups by 만 (ten-thousands), so 45,000 is '4 ten-thousands and 5 thousands.'

sections.title: Counters: 개, 명, 마리
sections.explanation: Korean requires counter words (분류사) when counting things — you cannot just say 'three apples' as a number plus a noun. Instead, you say noun + number + counter: 사과 세 개 (apple three-piece = three apples). The most common counters are: 개 — general counter for objects (like 'piece' or 'thing'); 명 — counter for people (polite: 분); 마리 — counter for animals. Other common counters include: 잔 (cups of drinks), 병 (bottles), 권 (books/volumes), 장 (flat things like paper), 대 (machines/vehicles), 벌 (sets of clothing). The number always uses the native Korean system with counters (except for specific cases like 층 for building floors, which uses Sino-Korean). Remember the shortened forms: 한 개, 두 명, 세 마리, 네 잔.
sections.tip: The basic pattern is: Noun + Native Korean Number + Counter. Start with 개 (things), 명 (people), 마리 (animals) — these three cover most daily counting situations.

examples.en: two apples (개 = general counter for things)

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'five books' using the counter 권?
exercises.explanation: The pattern is: 책 (book) + 다섯 (5, native Korean) + 권 (book counter) = 책 다섯 권. Counters use native Korean numbers, not Sino-Korean.

exercises.prompt: Which counter would you use for dogs?
exercises.explanation: 마리 is the counter for animals: 강아지 두 마리 (two dogs). 명 is for people, and 개 is the general counter for objects (not animals).

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'four people' politely?
exercises.explanation: 분 is the polite counter for people (명 is neutral). 네 is the shortened form of 넷 (4) before a counter. So 'four people' (polite) = 네 분.
